content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901           322-6441              322-6444


March 15, 1983


Mr. Brian Carr
Office of the County Counsel
County Government Center, East Wing
70 West Hedding Street
San Jose, CA 95110

                                        Re: Our Advice No. A-83-046

Dear Mr. Carr:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on
penalty of County Supervisor Thomas Logan. Mr. Logan has asked
whether he can vote on a proposed hazardous waste ordinance for
Santa Clara County.

     According to your letter, Mr. Logan is employed by Kaiser
Cement, which is a source of income to him within the meaning of
Government Code Section 87103.1/  Kaiser Cement is one of the
half-dozen largest businesses in the unincorporated area of
Santa Clara County. The proposed ordinance would have a
material financial effect on Kaiser Cement, within the meaning
of Government Code Section 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702. The ordinance would also affect a "great many" other
businesses in the unincorporated area, although the number of
businesses which would be affected, and the potential extent of
the effect on each business, is unknown at this time.

                            CONCLUSION

     It is our advice, based upon the facts you have provided,
that Mr. Logan should disqualify himself from voting on, or
participating in consideration of, the ordinance.


_____________________

     1/  All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

Mr. Brian Carr
March 15, 1983
Page 2


ANALYSIS

As a public official Mr. Legan must disqualify himself from
participation in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguish-
able from its effect on the public generally, upon a source of
income to him as defined in Section 87103. Since you have
stated that Kaiser Cement is a source of income to Mr. Legan,
and that the proposed ordinance would have a material financial
effect on Kaiser, Mr. Legan must disqualify himself from all
consideration of the ordinance unless the effect of the ordinance
on Kaiser would not be distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally. Section 87103.

For the "public generally" exception to apply to this
situation, the ordinance would have to affect a "significant
segment" of the public, and it would have to affect Kaiser
Cement "in substantially the same manner" as it would affect the
other members of that segment of the public. 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18703. On the basis of the facts you have provided, I
cannot say that either aspect of this test is met.

It is undoubtedly true that a great many oussinesses will be
affected by the ordinance; however, "a great many" businesses do
not necessarily constitute a significant segment of the public.2/
There will certainly be a large number of businesses which will
not be affected at all by the ordinance. Although there are no
firm guidelines for determining what constitutes a significant
segment of the public, the Commission has considered the public
generally issue on several occasions. It has held that 50% of
all commercial property owners do not constitute a significant
segment of the public generally. Brown Opinion, 4 PPC 19, 23
(1978). Owners of four or more units of rental housing do not
constitute a significant segment of the public. Ferraro
Opinion, 4 PPPC 62 (1978). On the other hand, all retail
business establishments in a core area of the City of Davis
were held to constitute a significant segment of the public
generally. Owen Opinion, 2 PPPC 77 (1976). Based on these
decisions, and absent more complete facts, it appears most
likely that the number of businesses which will be affected by
the ordinance is not sufficient to constitute a significant
segment of the public.

___________________________

2/ You have stated that Kaiser is not part of the
"predominant industry" in the county. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18703(C).

Mr. Brian Carr
March 15, 1983
Page 3


     There is also some doubt as to whether the second half of
the public generally test is met. Even if enough businesses to
constitute a significant segment of the public generally were
affected by the ordinance, Mr. Legan would have to disqualify
himself unless Kaiser Cement were affected "in substantially the
same manner" as the rest of the businesses affected. Again,
without more facts, we cannot be certain how the effect on
Kaiser would compare with the effects on other businesses.
However, given the fact that Kaiser is one of the half-dozen
largest businesses in the area, and the fact that the ordinance
imposes different types of requirements on different types of
hazardous wastes, it seems most likely that the effect on Kaiser
will differ substantially, both in kind and in magnitude, from
the effect on other businesses.

     The facts as you have stated them are clearly not sufficient
to establish that the public generally exception applies to
Mr. Legan's situation. On the contrary, on the basis of the
given facts, it seems most likely that the effect of the pro-
posed ordinance on Kaiser Cement will be distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally. Therefore our advice is
that Mr. Legan should disqualify himself from participation in
consideration of the proposed ordinance. Of course, it is
possible that, given more complete facts, our advice could
change. We would be happy to reconsider your question if you
want to make a fuller factual presentation.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief, Legal Division

BAM:km

Office of the County Counsel
County Government Center
70 West Hedding Street
San Jose, California 95110
299-2111 Area Code 408

County of Santa Clara

California                 February 16, 1983        Donald L. Clark, County Counsel

                          FEB 22 9 07 AM '83


Ms. Barbara Milman
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
P.O. Box 307
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Ms. Milman:

     Pursuant to our telephone conversation last week, we are
herein requesting written advice from the Fair Political
Practices Commission as provided by Government Code §
3114(b). Our question is whether County Supervisor Thomas
Legan, who is employed by Kaiser Cement, can participate in
the decision making process involving a proposed hazardous
materials ordinance.

     For the purposes of this letter, we ask you to assume that
the ordinance would have a material effect on Kaiser, and that
Kaiser is a "financial interest" as defined by Government Code
§ 87103. Therefore, we believe the key question is whether
the effect on Kaiser is "distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally." (§ 87103)

     In responding to this question, we understand you may be
guided by 2 Cal. Admin. Code § 18703 which defines the phrase
"effect on the public generally." Under that regulation,
where the financial interest is included within the "pre-
dominant" industry in the jurisdiction, it would be considered
a "significant segment" of the public and there would be no
prohibition on participation in the decision making process.
Although cement production is not the predominant industry in
this jurisdiction, the hazardous waste ordinance would apply
to all industry in the jurisdiction. The effect of the or-
dinance is therefore even more general than if it applied only
to a "predominant" industry.

     Our understanding is that besides Kaiser (which is among
the half-dozen largest businesses in the unincorporated area)
there are a great many businesses in the area which would be
materially affected by the ordinance. More detailed informa-
tion on the effect on Kaiser and other businesses is not
available at this time.




                    An Equal Opportunity Employer

Ms. Barbara Milman                    -2-                    February 16, 1983


     We believe your answer to this question may determine the
degree to which individuals in the business community can take
part in governmental decisions affecting the general com-
munity.  We therefore await your response with considerable
interest.

     Should you need further information, please feel free to
contact me.


                                   Very truly yours,

                                   DONALD L. CLARK
                                   County Counsel

                                   [signature]
                                   BRIAN L. CARR
                                   Deputy County  Counsel


BLC:mw

cc:  Supervisor Thomas Logan

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322 5660        322 5901        322 6441        322 6444


February 23, 1983


Brian L. Care
Deputy County Counsel
County Government Center, East Wing
70 W. Hedding Street
San Jose, CA 95110

                                                                Re: A-83-046

Dear Mr. Care:

    Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

    We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                                Very truly yours,


                                                Barbara A. Milman
                                                Chief
                                                Legal Division

BAM:km

sections.question_synthetic: Can County Supervisor Thomas Legan participate in a decision on a proposed hazardous waste ordinance when he is employed by Kaiser Cement, a company that would be materially affected by the ordinance?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Legan must disqualify himself from voting on and participating in consideration of the hazardous waste ordinance because the ordinance would have a material financial effect on Kaiser Cement that is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (February 16, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (March 15, 1983). The Question section was synthesized from the stated facts and context in the FPPC's response letter. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not significantly affect meaning.

QUESTION: Mr. Legan has asked whether he can vote on a proposed hazardous waste ordinance for Santa Clara County. According to your letter, Mr. Legan is employed by Kaiser Cement, which is a source of income to him within the meaning of Government Code Section 87103. Kaiser Cement is among the half-dozen largest businesses in the unincorporated area of Santa Clara County. The proposed ordinance would have a material financial effect on Kaiser Cement, within the meaning of Government Code Section 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

CONCLUSION: It is our advlce, based upon the facts you have provided, 
that Mr. Legan should disqualify himself from voting on, or 
participatlng in consideration of, the ordinance. 
11 
All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise seated. 


Mr. Brian Carr 
Ma r ch 15, 198 3

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that County Supervisor Thomas Legan, who is employed by Kaiser Cement, must disqualify himself from voting on and participating in a proposed hazardous waste ordinance for Santa Clara Clara County because the ordinance would have a material financial effect on his employer that does not meet the 'public generally' exception.
